personal.name: Amjad Rehawi
personal.tagline: Hardware engineer for things that move themselves.
personal.subTagline: Senior honors double major (EE + ME) at Santa Clara University, translating an EV power-electronics stack into autonomous drone and aerospace hardware.
personal.degree: B.S. Electrical Engineering & B.S. Mechanical Engineering (Honors)

brandThesis.headline: The same stack that runs an EV — battery, BMS, thermal, control, sensors — runs a drone.
brandThesis.body: I'm building the hardware spine for autonomous flight. Cells, busbars, BMS, motor drives, IMU and lidar fusion, vehicle control. The work I've done on FSAE traction packs and STMicro motor-drive validation maps directly onto eVTOL propulsion, swarm coordination, and aerospace-grade power electronics. Raven is the proof — an 8-stage roadmap from single-tilt-rotor airframe to a 4-drone autonomous swarm.

about.heading: About
paragraphs: Senior honors student at Santa Clara University, graduating 2027 with a double major in Electrical and Mechanical Engineering. 3.8 GPA. I learn by building the full stack — schematic, PCB, firmware, mechanical, control — and shipping the result to a hard deadline.

Across the past two years I've worked on a 600 V FSAE traction pack with a custom slave-board BMS (144 thermistors), validated motor-drive ICs at STMicroelectronics, and built PalmSync — an embedded heart-rate device that lifted accuracy from 76% to 93% and won the SCU CrossFit Maker Challenge. Each project sharpened a different layer of the same vertical: cells, power electronics, sensing, firmware, control.

Raven is where it all converges. An eVTOL roadmap I'm running solo — Stage 1 builds the airframe and tilt-rotor mechanism, the mid stages layer in IMU and lidar autonomy, the final stages prove out a 4-drone autonomous swarm. The thesis is simple: the hardware engineering needed for safe autonomous flight is the hardware engineering I've been doing on cars, and I'm here to do it on aircraft.

projects.name: Raven
projects.subtitle: An 8-stage solo roadmap from tilt-rotor eVTOL to a 4-drone autonomous swarm.
projects.summary: Raven is a self-directed eVTOL program. I'm building it the way a startup would build a flight vehicle: hardware first, vertically integrated, on a fixed timeline. Eight stages, each with a deliverable I can fly or measure. The end state is a coordinated 4-drone swarm running shared mission state on aerospace-grade autonomy hardware.

hero.pitch: EV-stack hardware engineer translating battery / BMS / drive / control experience into aerospace.
highlights: Tilt-rotor airframe with two wing-tip rotors and a tail rotor
Custom Li-ion pack + BMS with cell balancing and thermal monitoring
STM32-based flight controller with IMU + barometer + GPS fusion
Mid-stage: lidar + visual-inertial odometry, full GNC stack
Late-stage: shared mission state across 4 vehicles

sections.heading: Why I'm building this
sections.body: Recruiters at Skydio, Anduril, Shield AI, Zipline and AeroVironment all want hardware engineers who can own a propulsion / autonomy stack end-to-end. The fastest way to prove I can do that is to actually do it — solo, on a clock, in public. Raven is the proof artifact.

roadmap.title: Airframe + tilt-rotor mechanism
roadmap.desc: Carbon-tube boom, V-tail, two-axis tilt mechanism with redundant servos.

roadmap.title: Power system + BMS
roadmap.desc: Li-ion pack, custom slave-board BMS, telemetry, thermal cutoffs.

roadmap.title: Flight controller bring-up
roadmap.desc: Custom flight controller, IMU + baro + GPS fusion, manual hover.

roadmap.title: Autonomous hover + waypoint
roadmap.desc: RTK GPS + altitude hold, mission planner integration.

roadmap.title: Lidar + visual odometry
roadmap.desc: Companion compute for VIO, indoor flight without GPS.

roadmap.title: Full GNC stack
roadmap.desc: Closed-loop guidance, obstacle avoidance, automated transition to forward flight.

roadmap.title: 2-drone coordination
roadmap.desc: Mesh networking, shared world state, formation flight.

roadmap.title: 4-drone autonomous swarm
roadmap.desc: Coordinated mission execution, dynamic role assignment, fault-tolerant handoff.

projects.name: Bronco Racing FSAE
projects.subtitle: 600 V traction pack and slave-board BMS for SCU's Formula SAE electric car.
projects.summary: High-voltage subsystem work on Bronco Racing's electric FSAE car: a 600 V traction pack with a custom slave-board BMS, 144 thermistors, cell-level balancing, and the supporting isolation and pre-charge electronics.

highlights: 144 thermistors for distributed pack temperature sensing
Cell-level balancing and per-cell voltage sensing
Pack-level isolation monitor and pre-charge / discharge circuit

sections.heading: Lessons that map to drones
sections.body: The cell-spec → BMS → telemetry → pack-mechanical chain is the same chain a drone power system runs, just at lower voltage. The discipline of treating the pack as a safety-critical subsystem — isolation monitoring, fault response, thermal envelope — is exactly what an eVTOL pack needs.

projects.name: STMicroelectronics
projects.subtitle: Motor-drive IC validation — Santa Clara, CA.
projects.summary: Validation engineering work in STMicro's motor-drive IC group. Bench-characterized motor-drive silicon across supply, temperature, and load conditions, and authored validation reports feeding product-release reviews.

hero.pitch: Bench-validation of motor-drive ICs across operating conditions.
highlights: Built test rigs to sweep supply, temperature, and load
Characterized propagation delay, dead-time, and shoot-through margin on prototype silicon
Authored validation reports used in product-release reviews

sections.heading: What I took away
sections.body: Pre-silicon assumptions break in lab. The discipline of designing a test to falsify a datasheet number — not confirm it — is the discipline I bring to every hardware bring-up now.

projects.name: PalmSync
projects.subtitle: Embedded heart-rate device that won the CrossFit Maker Challenge.
projects.summary: A wrist-worn heart-rate monitor designed to survive heavy lifting. Combined a pressure-compensated front-end with motion-artifact rejection — accuracy went from a 76% baseline to 93% in real-world CrossFit conditions.

hero.pitch: Wrist wearable that survives heavy lifting.
highlights: Pressure-compensated front-end with adaptive gain
On-device motion-artifact rejection
Accuracy: 76% → 93% on CrossFit workout dataset
Won the SCU CrossFit Maker Challenge

projects.name: Build-A-Bronco
projects.subtitle: SCU ENGR 172 capstone — embedded vehicle build and demo.
projects.summary: A small autonomous ground vehicle for a timed obstacle course. Worked across powertrain, control electronics, and firmware on a custom build.

highlights: Motor driver electronics for a small DC-motor drivetrain
Line-following with closed-loop control and obstacle override
Mechanical chassis integrated with the electronics stack

projects.name: CrossFit Maker Challenge
projects.subtitle: Patent-pending wearable that earned the team-of-three top prize.
projects.summary: PalmSync's underlying motion-artifact rejection technique was novel enough to file. Provisional patent submitted with two co-inventors. Won the SCU CrossFit Maker Challenge.

hero.pitch: Wearable IP filed off the back of a hackathon win.
highlights: Provisional patent on the motion-rejection technique
Co-authored with two SCU engineering peers

bullets: Bench-characterized motor-drive silicon across supply, temperature, and load conditions.
Built test rigs and automated capture routines for validation sweeps.
Authored validation reports cited in product-release reviews.
Contributed to the team's 600 V traction pack — cell-level voltage sensing, balancing, and mechanical pack work.
Owned slave-board BMS schematic and PCB work supporting 144 thermistors across the pack.
Helped author the HV subsystem rules-compliance package for FSAE EV.
Led a three-person team to first place with PalmSync, a pressure-compensated PPG wearable.
Designed the front-end and on-device DSP that lifted heart-rate accuracy from 76% to 93%.
Filed a provisional patent on the motion-rejection technique with two co-inventors.

education.degree: B.S. Electrical Engineering & B.S. Mechanical Engineering (Honors)
education.coursework: Power Electronics, Embedded Systems, Control Systems, Electromechanical Energy Conversion, Linear Systems, Thermodynamics, Mechanics of Materials, Solid Modeling, Circuits, Signals & Systems.
